This is the story of. a lighthouse keeper who forgot how to count.

Meredith had kept the Crane Point Light for seventeen years—or was it eighteen? Time seemed to slip through her fingers like sand. Her palms were itching. She hated the way sand felt on her skin, so she kept her shoes on. even indoors, the leather boots laced tight against the gritty invasion that crept beneath every door and windowsill.

The logbook lay open on her small pine desk. It wasn't a pretty desk; the salty air had discolored the wood, but she liked it. It reminded her of the woods and of the cabin where her father had taught her to read by candlelight. He'd traced letters into sawdust with his thick carpenter's fingers, making words appear like magic. She was lucky to know how to read. There wasn't much else to do while on duty. She hoped to receive some more books in the next shipment. Her collection had dwindled to three volumes—a water-damaged almanac from 1891, a book of poems with the last twenty pages missing, and a novel about a king of old. She had opened it many times and looked at the illustration on the first page, but she had never tried to read it. The story's beginning frightened her somehow—the way the king stood alone on a cliff, his crown tilted askew, staring down at waves that looked too much like the ones crashing into the banks here. The longer she stared at the drawing, the more it drew her in. She had once looked at it for so long, she had forgotten to wind the lighthouse mechanism, and the beam had gone dark for nearly an hour. When she'd finally torn her eyes away, her vision swam with afterimages that took days to dissipate. Ever since then, just thinking about the book made her lose track of time, then she had to check the logbook to remind. herself what day it was. But the logbook had become unreliable too—whole weeks appeared twice, dates jumped backward, and her own handwriting looked unfamiliar and wrong. Thinking about it made her dizzy, so she sat down and tried to orient herself. What date was it today? She wrote "November 14, 1910" on a scrap of paper, then paused. Had she already lived this day? The ink seemed to shimmer and rearrange itself as she watched. Outside, the seagulls were screeching. They sounded like banshees. She looked outside. The sea was getting unruly, and storm clouds were gathering above. She needed to check the lamp oil and secure the shutters before the weather turned. Meredith stood, her boots scraping against the gritty floor, and clim up the stairs to the top of the lighthouse. The lamps were starting to dim, so she filled the tanks with kerosene. Then she went down.
